items.label: Contraction mapping on a metric space

candidate_anchors.snippet: a map T : X → X is called a contraction mapping on X if there exists a nonnegative constant q < 1

items.rationale: The article explicitly introduces the definition of a contraction mapping, with both prose and a defining inequality.

items.label: Banach fixed-point theorem

candidate_anchors.snippet: Banach fixed-point theorem
Let (X, d) be a non-empty complete metric space with a contraction mapping T : X → X. Then T admits a unique fixed point

items.rationale: This is the main theorem statement: existence and uniqueness of a fixed point for a contraction on a complete metric space.

items.label: Picard iteration converges to the fixed point

candidate_anchors.snippet: start with an arbitrary element x_0 in X and define x_n = T(x_{n-1}) for n ≥ 1. Then lim_{n→∞} x_n = x*

items.rationale: The constructive part of the theorem — that Picard iterations converge to the fixed point — is a distinct claim usable independently.

items.label: Rate-of-convergence inequalities for Banach iteration

candidate_anchors.snippet: The following inequalities are equivalent and describe the speed of convergence

items.rationale: Remark 1 gives four explicit error/rate-of-convergence inequalities, a distinct proposition from the existence/uniqueness theorem.

items.label: Lipschitz constant (and best Lipschitz constant)

candidate_anchors.snippet: The value q is called a Lipschitz constant for T, and a minimal such q is sometimes called "the best Lipschitz constant" of T.

items.rationale: The article defines the Lipschitz constant and the best Lipschitz constant terminology.

items.label: Counter-example: T(x)=x+1/x on [1,∞) has no fixed point

candidate_anchors.snippet: The condition d(T(x),T(y))<d(x,y) for all x ≠ y is in general not enough to ensure the existence of a fixed point

items.rationale: A canonical counter-example showing strict contractivity alone is insufficient for existence of a fixed point.

items.label: Fixed-point theorem on compact metric spaces under strict contraction

candidate_anchors.snippet: if X is compact, then this weaker condition does imply the existence and uniqueness of a fixed point which can be easily found as a minimizer of d(x,T(x))

items.rationale: States a separate fixed-point existence/uniqueness result for compact metric spaces under the weaker strict-contraction condition.

items.label: Picard–Lindelöf theorem via Banach fixed-point

candidate_anchors.snippet: Picard–Lindelöf theorem

items.rationale: The article cites Picard–Lindelöf existence/uniqueness for ODEs as a consequence; named theorem.

items.label: Small Lipschitz perturbations of identity are bi-Lipschitz homeomorphisms

candidate_anchors.snippet: small Lipschitz perturbations of the identity are bi-lipschitz homeomorphisms
Let Ω be an open set of a Banach space E; let I:Ω→E denote the identity (inclusion) map and let g:Ω→E be a Lipschitz map of constant k<1

items.rationale: A precisely stated proposition with hypotheses and conclusions about I+g being a bi-Lipschitz homeomorphism.

items.label: Openness of (I+g)Ω under small Lipschitz perturbation

candidate_anchors.snippet: Ω':=(I+g)Ω is an open subset of E

items.rationale: The first numbered subclaim — explicit openness statement with a quantitative ball inclusion — is a distinct proposition.

items.label: Inverse of I+g is of the form I+h with Lipschitz constant k/(1-k)

candidate_anchors.snippet: (I+g)^{-1} is still of the form I+h:Ω→Ω' with h a Lipschitz map of constant k/(1-k)

items.rationale: Distinct quantitative claim about the inverse map's form and Lipschitz constant.

items.label: Inverse function theorem (via Banach FPT)

candidate_anchors.snippet: inverse function theorem

items.rationale: Named theorem stated as a direct consequence; a distinct, formalizable item.

items.label: Nash embedding theorem (via Banach FPT)

candidate_anchors.snippet: Nash embedding theorem

items.rationale: Named theorem referenced as obtainable via Banach FPT.

items.label: Map-on-floor illustration of the theorem

candidate_anchors.snippet: Suppose a person holds a sheet of people on which is printed a map of the city they are in

items.rationale: An informal real-world example demonstrating the theorem's conclusion.

items.label: Bessaga converse of the Banach contraction principle

candidate_anchors.snippet: Let f:X→X be a map of an abstract set such that each iterate f^n has a unique fixed point. Let q ∈ (0, 1), then there exists a complete metric on X such that f is contractive, and q is the contraction constant.

items.rationale: A named converse (Bessaga, 1959) with explicit hypotheses and conclusion.

items.label: Hitzler–Seda converse on T1 topological spaces

candidate_anchors.snippet: if f : X → X is a map on a T1 topological space with a unique fixed point a, such that for each x ∈ X we have f^n(x)→a, then there already exists a metric on X with respect to which f satisfies the conditions of the Banach contraction principle with contraction constant 1/2

items.rationale: A distinct converse result with weaker (T1) hypotheses, plus the ultrametric strengthening.

items.label: Iterate-is-contraction generalization

candidate_anchors.snippet: Assume that some iterate T^n of T is a contraction. Then T has a unique fixed point.

items.rationale: A standard generalization: contraction of an iterate suffices for unique fixed point.

items.label: Summable Lipschitz-coefficients generalization

candidate_anchors.snippet: Assume that for each n, there exist c_n such that d(T^n(x),T^n(y))≤c_n d(x,y) for all x and y, and that

items.rationale: A separate generalization based on summability of per-iterate Lipschitz constants.

items.label: Edelstein-type theorem on compact metric spaces

candidate_anchors.snippet: In a non-empty compact metric space, any function T satisfying d(T(x),T(y))<d(x,y) for all distinct x,y, has a unique fixed point.

items.rationale: Explicit existence/uniqueness statement for strict contractions on compact metric spaces.

items.label: Approximating π by iterating f(x)=x+sin(x)

candidate_anchors.snippet: An application of the Banach fixed-point theorem and fixed-point iteration can be used to quickly obtain an approximation of π with high accuracy. Consider the function f(x)=x+sin(x).

items.rationale: A concrete worked example using Banach FPT to compute π via iteration of x+sin x.

items.label: Lipschitz bound 0 ≤ 1+cos(x) ≤ 1−1/√2 on [3π/4, 5π/4]
items.rationale: A quantitative inequality used to certify the Lipschitz constant in the π example; distinct enough to formalize separately.
